zotero.abstractNote: Object-centric learning aims to decompose scenes into individual objects, which is widely believed to be a prerequisite for compositional generalization. However, current object-centric models often fail to generalize to novel combinations of objects, even when they achieve high reconstruction quality. In this paper, we provide a theoretical framework to analyze the compositional generalization of object-centric models. We identify a fundamental identifiability gap: even if a model perfectly reconstructs the training data, it may fail to learn the true underlying causal factors of the scene. We prove that this gap can be closed by enforcing a specific form of consistency across different scenes. Based on this, we propose a novel training objective that provably improves compositional generalization. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on several synthetic and real-world datasets, showing that it significantly outperforms existing methods in terms of compositional generalization.
zotero.proceedingsTitle: The Twelfth International Conference on Learning Representations
